title: Flax-fibre handmade paper
materials: retted flax (dried flax straw soaked and retted for 2-4 weeks) OR prepared flax fibre
soda ash (sodium carbonate) for cooking the raw fibre
sources: Flax fibre papermaking documented in Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923) — public domain. Early European paper was predominantly linen rag (flax); the long-fibre nature of flax produces a strong, smooth sheet.
notes: European paper before wood-pulp was mostly linen rag (which is reted flax fibre). This tutorial covers making paper from raw flax fibre (not rag): the cook-and-beat sequence before sheet forming. Covers: sourcing flax straw (garden or craft supplier), the cooking (sodium carbonate at a simmer for 2-4 hours until the bast fibre separates), blending the cooked fibre (shorter beats produce rougher paper; longer beats produce smoother), the effect of long flax fibres on drainage (slower than cotton), couching and drying. Resulting paper is stronger and more textile-like than cotton-linter paper. Differentiate from hemp (similar process) and kozo washi (which uses nagashizuki technique).
